SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: The application outlines a robust dissemination plan including open-access publications, Plain English Summaries co-developed with PPI contributors, and co-authorship/co-presentation at events. There are clear links between objectives (e.g., digital stratification tool) and anticipated benefits (timely intervention, equitable access, improved patient autonomy). The focus on actionable outcomes is strong, aiming to change clinical pathways for psoriasis management.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the plan is specific, costed, and directly aligned with patient priorities.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the plan is specific, costed, and directly aligned with patient priorities.

learning approaches. External validation will be
performed in diverse datasets.
Study 3 for Objective 3 (11 – 36 months): This qualitative study will use focus groups with people with
psoriasis to explore attitudes towards communication of psoriasis disease burden. Focus groups with
healthcare professionals will explore attitudes towards patient self-assessment and tool deployment.
Insights will inform a communication interface prototype.
Impact:
A patient-held digital stratification tool for self-assessment of current and predicted psoriasis disease
burden could enable timely intervention, greater patient autonomy and improved outcomes, through
equitable access to care in line with NICE guidelines. This project aligns with NIHR James Lind Alliance
research priorities and the NHS Long Term Plan to support digitally-driven, proactive care.
Dissemination:
Findings will be published in open-access journals and presented at national/international conferences,
with co-developed Plain English Summaries for international multi-media dissemination via our
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
established networks.

results back to
individuals.
Working with People and Communities: This project addresses key research priorities highlighted by
people with psoriasis. People with psoriasis have directly shaped this project. There was encouraging
support for a digital tool to support a personalised treatment approach in psoriasis using health and
genetic information. People with psoriasis and the public will advise on this project through 6-monthly
meetings. They will help plan and analyse the group discussions. They will also ensure this research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact/Dissemination: This project will support people with psoriasis to get equal access to the right
care at the right time, and improve long-term outcomes. It will allow healthcare professionals to provide
better care, tailored to individual needs to improve patient experience. I will share my findings in
scientific journals and at national/ international conferences. I will work with people with psoriasis to
create easily understood materials for circulation via multiple media platforms.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets diverse groups (age, ethnicity, sex, location) in recruitment and PLAG formation. Specific strategies include leveraging the Psoriasis Association, using diverse ancestry datasets (Our Future Health, East London Genes & Health), and mitigating digital exclusion via phone-in options. The budget includes specific lines for diverse recruitment and accessibility measures.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While barriers like digital exclusion are addressed, there is less detail on specific barriers faced by other underserved groups (e.g., language barriers, specific socioeconomic constraints beyond travel reimbursement) and how they will be mitigated beyond general diversity statements.
sub_criteria.rationale: While barriers like digital exclusion are addressed, there is less detail on specific barriers faced by other underserved groups (e.g., language barriers, specific socioeconomic constraints beyond travel reimbursement) and how they will be mitigated beyond general diversity statements.

evidence.text:
liable digital tool that allows people with psoriasis to assess and predict if
they have or will go on to develop severe psoriasis.
Methods: First, I will recruit people with psoriasis across the UK from a variety of settings and
backgrounds (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and location) and invite them to self-assess how severe their
psoriasis is, on the mySkin app, using health information. I will invite 50 people from this group for an in-
person review by a skin specialist to test if they agree with the self-assessment.
Then, using large datasets of diverse populations, I will use different types of mathematical modelling to
predict who is likely to develop severe psoriasis. I will test and refine these predictions to improve my
model. This will be integrated onto a digital tool in the future.
Finally, I will host group discussions with people with psoriasis (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and
background) to understand how to communicate if a person has or will develop severe psoriasis back to
the individual. I will have group discussions with healthcare professionals to understand their views on
the tool. I will use this feedback to develop a preliminary platform to communicate

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusion is demonstrated across the entire lifecycle: co-development of the proposal, shaping recruitment strategies, involvement in data interpretation (PLAG members trained in qualitative analysis), and co-dissemination. Accessibility is considered through hybrid meeting options and phone-in alternatives. The plan includes ongoing evaluation via biannual meetings and key decision points.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation is strong but relies heavily on the PLAG meetings; there is no explicit mention of a formal feedback loop mechanism for participants outside the PLAG to adapt the study in real-time if issues arise.
sub_criteria.rationale: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation is strong but relies heavily on the PLAG meetings; there is no explicit mention of a formal feedback loop mechanism for participants outside the PLAG to adapt the study in real-time if issues arise.

sub_criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the budget is comprehensive and directly supports the inclusion activities described.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the budget is comprehensive and directly supports the inclusion activities described.

criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

criteria.pros: The application provides exceptional evidence of co-design activities (webinar, survey, engagement group) that directly shaped the research design. Specific examples of changes are detailed: the decision to use a digital tool (78% support), inclusion of genetic data (96% support), and the focus on communication of results (90% support) were all driven by patient feedback. A Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG) is established to guide recruitment, data interpretation, and dissemination.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive, specific, and directly links patient input to study design modifications.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive, specific, and directly links patient input to study design modifications.

evidence.text:
This proposal directly addresses five of the top 20 NIHR James Lind Alliance psoriasis research
priorities related to timely intervention and disease progression, to address significant unmet need.
I have co-developed all stages of my project alongside people with psoriasis, their carers and my patient
collaborator, Helen McAteer (Chief Executive, Psoriasis Association, the UK’s leading psoriasis patient
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
organisation) with diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, geographical location and psoriasis severity through:
• A 30 minute national Psoriasis Association webinar (n=69 attendees; 193 YouTube views)
• A national survey of people with psoriasis (n=73)
• A 1.5 hour in-person engagement group with people with psoriasis and their carers (n=11)
From these activities, four individuals with psoriasis (diverse in age, sex and ethnicity) have expressed
an interest in joining my Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG; total n=6). Additional recruitment will be
via the Psoriasis Association and our established PPI network to ensure diversity in views, background
and experience. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings at key decision points (in-
person

/virtual to maximise inclusivity). To reduce digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and
provide phone-in options. All collaborators will be offered formal training via Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS
Trust and the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. Their input will shape
recruitment, data interpretation and dissemination. They will ensure my research is relevant, inclusive
and equitable.
All participants in my engagement group and survey strongly supported my research question and felt it
was relevant. They emphasised: a) accessing timely intervention for psoriasis is a challenge and b)
personalised strategies based on psoriasis disease burden, co-designed with patients, are preferable to
a generic approach.
This work has shaped my proposal. Participants in my engagement group were enthusiastic about a
digital stratification tool to assess current and predicted psoriasis disease burden. In my survey:
- 78% were willing to provide health information using a digital tool (smartphone app) for personalised
psoriasis management advice, shaping the self-assessment approach using health information on
mySkin in Objective 1. I will leverage the Psori

asis Association and my PLAG to inform diverse and
inclusive recruitment strategies to do this.
- 96% supported the use of genetic data for personalised psoriasis management advice, shaping the
inclusion of genetic information in my model in Objective 2. My PLAG will help to interpret model results.
External validation in diverse datasets will ensure generalisability and mitigate health inequity.
- 90% of people with psoriasis wanted to know their current and future psoriasis disease burden,
shaping the multistakeholder focus groups to understand and optimise this communication through a
digital stratification tool in Objective 3. PLAG members will receive qualitative research training, recruit
diverse focus groups participants (age, ethnicity, location) and interpret findings.
PPI contributors will receive training to co-develop Plain English summaries for multi-media
dissemination, co-author peer-reviewed publications and co-present at national events. PPI contributors
will be reimbursed in line with NIHR guidance. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI
activities.

criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

criteria.pros: The supervisory team is diverse in expertise (epidemiology, genetics, statistics, clinical dermatology) and includes a patient collaborator (Helen McAteer). The PLAG is explicitly recruited for diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, and location.
criteria.drawbacks: While the PLAG and supervisors show diversity, there is limited explicit detail on the demographic composition of the core research team (e.g., the applicant and supervisors' own demographics) beyond their professional roles. Task allocation is described generally but not explicitly linked to equity principles within the team structure.
criteria.rationale: While the PLAG and supervisors show diversity, there is limited explicit detail on the demographic composition of the core research team (e.g., the applicant and supervisors' own demographics) beyond their professional roles. Task allocation is described generally but not explicitly linked to equity principles within the team structure.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

criteria.pros: The application outlines a robust dissemination plan including open-access publications, Plain English Summaries co-developed with PPI contributors, and co-authorship/co-presentation at events. There are clear links between objectives (e.g., digital stratification tool) and anticipated benefits (timely intervention, equitable access, improved patient autonomy). The focus on actionable outcomes is strong, aiming to change clinical pathways for psoriasis management.
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the plan is specific, costed, and directly aligned with patient priorities.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the plan is specific, costed, and directly aligned with patient priorities.

evidence.text:
learning approaches. External validation will be
performed in diverse datasets.
Study 3 for Objective 3 (11 – 36 months): This qualitative study will use focus groups with people with
psoriasis to explore attitudes towards communication of psoriasis disease burden. Focus groups with
healthcare professionals will explore attitudes towards patient self-assessment and tool deployment.
Insights will inform a communication interface prototype.
Impact:
A patient-held digital stratification tool for self-assessment of current and predicted psoriasis disease
burden could enable timely intervention, greater patient autonomy and improved outcomes, through
equitable access to care in line with NICE guidelines. This project aligns with NIHR James Lind Alliance
research priorities and the NHS Long Term Plan to support digitally-driven, proactive care.
Dissemination:
Findings will be published in open-access journals and presented at national/international conferences,
with co-developed Plain English Summaries for international multi-media dissemination via our
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
established networks.

results back to
individuals.
Working with People and Communities: This project addresses key research priorities highlighted by
people with psoriasis. People with psoriasis have directly shaped this project. There was encouraging
support for a digital tool to support a personalised treatment approach in psoriasis using health and
genetic information. People with psoriasis and the public will advise on this project through 6-monthly
meetings. They will help plan and analyse the group discussions. They will also ensure this research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact/Dissemination: This project will support people with psoriasis to get equal access to the right
care at the right time, and improve long-term outcomes. It will allow healthcare professionals to provide
better care, tailored to individual needs to improve patient experience. I will share my findings in
scientific journals and at national/ international conferences. I will work with people with psoriasis to
create easily understood materials for circulation via multiple media platforms.

criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets diverse groups (age, ethnicity, sex, location) in recruitment and PLAG formation. Specific strategies include leveraging the Psoriasis Association, using diverse ancestry datasets (Our Future Health, East London Genes & Health), and mitigating digital exclusion via phone-in options. The budget includes specific lines for diverse recruitment and accessibility measures.
criteria.drawbacks: While barriers like digital exclusion are addressed, there is less detail on specific barriers faced by other underserved groups (e.g., language barriers, specific socioeconomic constraints beyond travel reimbursement) and how they will be mitigated beyond general diversity statements.
criteria.rationale: While barriers like digital exclusion are addressed, there is less detail on specific barriers faced by other underserved groups (e.g., language barriers, specific socioeconomic constraints beyond travel reimbursement) and how they will be mitigated beyond general diversity statements.

evidence.text:
liable digital tool that allows people with psoriasis to assess and predict if
they have or will go on to develop severe psoriasis.
Methods: First, I will recruit people with psoriasis across the UK from a variety of settings and
backgrounds (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and location) and invite them to self-assess how severe their
psoriasis is, on the mySkin app, using health information. I will invite 50 people from this group for an in-
person review by a skin specialist to test if they agree with the self-assessment.
Then, using large datasets of diverse populations, I will use different types of mathematical modelling to
predict who is likely to develop severe psoriasis. I will test and refine these predictions to improve my
model. This will be integrated onto a digital tool in the future.
Finally, I will host group discussions with people with psoriasis (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and
background) to understand how to communicate if a person has or will develop severe psoriasis back to
the individual. I will have group discussions with healthcare professionals to understand their views on
the tool. I will use this feedback to develop a preliminary platform to communicate

criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

criteria.pros: Inclusion is demonstrated across the entire lifecycle: co-development of the proposal, shaping recruitment strategies, involvement in data interpretation (PLAG members trained in qualitative analysis), and co-dissemination. Accessibility is considered through hybrid meeting options and phone-in alternatives. The plan includes ongoing evaluation via biannual meetings and key decision points.
criteria.drawbacks: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation is strong but relies heavily on the PLAG meetings; there is no explicit mention of a formal feedback loop mechanism for participants outside the PLAG to adapt the study in real-time if issues arise.
criteria.rationale: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation is strong but relies heavily on the PLAG meetings; there is no explicit mention of a formal feedback loop mechanism for participants outside the PLAG to adapt the study in real-time if issues arise.

criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

criteria.pros: The budget explicitly details costs for PLAG meetings (£4,800), co-facilitation of focus groups (£600), training for PPI collaborators (£160), and participant payments for focus groups and clinical validation. Staff time and materials are clearly allocated. The resources align perfectly with the inclusion plan described in the PPI section (reimbursement in line with NIHR guidance, training provided).
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the budget is comprehensive and directly supports the inclusion activities described.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the budget is comprehensive and directly supports the inclusion activities described.

evidence.text:
asis Association and my PLAG to inform diverse and
inclusive recruitment strategies to do this.
- 96% supported the use of genetic data for personalised psoriasis management advice, shaping the
inclusion of genetic information in my model in Objective 2. My PLAG will help to interpret model results.
External validation in diverse datasets will ensure generalisability and mitigate health inequity.
- 90% of people with psoriasis wanted to know their current and future psoriasis disease burden,
shaping the multistakeholder focus groups to understand and optimise this communication through a
digital stratification tool in Objective 3. PLAG members will receive qualitative research training, recruit
diverse focus groups participants (age, ethnicity, location) and interpret findings.
PPI contributors will receive training to co-develop Plain English summaries for multi-media
dissemination, co-author peer-reviewed publications and co-present at national events. PPI contributors
will be reimbursed in line with NIHR guidance. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI
activities.

ion) Knowledge into Action Course £4,000 (£3,390 + travel/accommodation) Introduction to Implementation Science £1,400 • Research Inclusion (£29,600) Data access and management for large-scale diverse ancestry datasets for Year 2/3 - Our Future Health (£10,800/year) and East London Genes & Health (£4,000/year) • PPIE • Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG) Meetings (£4,800) Funds for 6 participants to meet for 2 hours (in-person) at 6-monthly intervals with 2 additional meetings at key decision points (£50pp/meeting + £50 pp/meeting for travel reimbursement and refreshments for 8 times during the fellowship, in line with NIHR guidance) • PLAG co-facilitation of qualitative focus groups (£600) A trained PLAG member will cofacilitate each focus group for Objective 3 (6 sessions upto 2 hours in- person/online; £50pp/session + £50pp for travel reimbursement and refreshments in line with NIHR guidance) • Qualitative data analysis training for PPIE collaborators (£160) Half day online training session two PPIE collaborators (£75 + £5 connection charge, in line with NIHR guidance) • Qualitative Focus Groups (£3,600) Payment for qualitative focus groups for Objective 3 (36 individuals for

upto 2 hours online/in person at £50pp + £50pp travel reimbursement + refreshments in line with NIHR guidance) • Payment for in-person clinical validation (£2,500) Payment for in-person clinical validation for Objective 1 (Total 50 participants for upto 1 hour in person at £25pp + £25pp travel reimbursement, in line with NIHR guidance) • Other Direct Costs NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) • Communication interface protoype development (£9,000) Funds have been requested for the communication interface prototype development in Objective 3 • GSTT NHS Foundation Trust Archiving Fee (£565) An archiving fee of £565 has been quoted by the GSTT NHS Foundation Trust as part of their role in facilitating participant recruitment for the in-person clinical validation and qualitative work. • Dictaphone (£475) For qualitative focus groups • Focus group transcription costs (£1,206) Funds to cover transcription costs for focus groups (6 focus groups each upto 2 hours, at £201.00 for 2 hours) • UK BioBank Dataset Access and Management in Year 2/3 (£7200) • Data storage, back up and security costs in Year 2/3 (£5000) • Indirect Costs • Estates (£96,917.70) HEI estate costs calculated by the KCL Costing

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings

Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

findings.implication: Scientific Abstract: Contains clear problem statement, objectives, methods, and impact, supporting pr.1.
Scientific Abstract: Identifies knowledge gaps regarding inequitable access and need for predictive tools, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Defines three studies with specific timelines (0-36 months), supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Describes cross-sectional validation, machine learning, and qualitative focus groups, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Highlights benefits like timely intervention, patient autonomy, and alignment with NHS Long Term Plan, supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: States proposal shaped by patients/carers and includes focus groups, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions ensuring inclusive research for diverse communities and external validation in diverse datasets, supporting pr.9.
Scientific Abstract: Notes proposal shaped by people with psoriasis and includes focus groups for design, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Plans open-access publications and Plain English Summaries for dissemination, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly mentions ensuring research is relevant to diverse communities, supporting wp.4.
Scientific Abstract: Describes involvement of stakeholders throughout the project lifecycle, supporting wp.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly outlines background, aim, methods, and impact in accessible language, supporting pr.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Identifies a specific knowledge gap regarding the lack of reliable tools to predict severe psoriasis, supporting pr.2.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes a feasible three-stage approach (recruitment, modelling, feedback) appropriate for the award scope, supporting pr.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details specific methods including self-assessment, specialist validation, mathematical modelling, and group discussions, supporting pr.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly states benefits for equal access to care, improved outcomes, and tailored patient experience, supporting pr.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes co-design of the app, 6-monthly advisory meetings, and involvement in planning/analysis, supporting pr.6.
Plain English Summary of Research: Commits to recruiting diverse populations and ensuring research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities, supporting pr.9.
Plain English Summary of Research: States the project addresses priorities highlighted by people with psoriasis and they have directly shaped the project, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Plans to share findings in journals, conferences, and create easily understood materials for the public, supporting wp.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly mentions recruiting diverse groups (age, ethnicity, sex, location) and ensuring relevance to diverse communities, supporting wp.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes involvement of people with psoriasis in planning, analysis, and ensuring inclusivity across the project, supporting wp.5.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details co-design activities (webinar, survey, engagement group) and a PLAG shaping recruitment, data interpretation, and dissemination, strongly supporting pr.6.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment (age, sex, ethnicity, location) and mitigating digital exclusion via phone-in options support pr.9.
Patient & Public Involvement: Specific examples of design changes based on patient feedback (digital tool, genetic data inclusion, communication focus) directly support wp.1.
Patient & Public Involvement: Plans for PPI contributors to co-develop Plain English summaries, co-author publications, and co-present at events support wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicit mention of recruiting diverse focus group participants (age, ethnicity, location) and ensuring diversity in the PLAG supports wp.4.
Patient & Public Involvement: Describes involvement across all stages (co-development, recruitment, data interpretation, dissemination) supporting wp.5.
Patient & Public Involvement: Mentions reimbursement in line with NIHR guidance and formal training for collaborators, supporting wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides detailed cost breakdowns with explicit justifications for staff, equipment, training, and PPIE activities, directly supporting pr.7.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Lists specific training courses (e.g., Epidemiology, R, Qualitative Methods) with costs and timelines, supporting td.1.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Maps training activities to project needs (e.g., statistical computing for modelling, qualitative methods for focus groups), supporting td.2.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Includes leadership courses (King's Fund, Future Leaders Academy) and implementation science training, supporting career aspirations in td.3.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Allocates specific funds for PLAG meetings, co-facilitation, and training, confirming a costed plan for inclusion in wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Budgets for diverse ancestry datasets (Our Future Health, East London Genes & Health) and UK BioBank, supporting inclusive design in pr.9.
Application Context: Describes a clear career trajectory from pre-doctoral to postdoctoral research via this fellowship, supported by a strong mentorship team, addressing g.1.
Application Context: Mentions the award as a transition to postdoctoral research and building on pre-doctoral experience, supporting g.2.
Application Context: Details applicant's background in dermatology, NIHR pathways, and prior work on the mySkin platform, aligning experience with project needs for g.3.
Application Context: Lists prior grants (MRC, NIHR Advanced Fellowship) and leadership roles (co-director of registries), indicating impactful outputs for g.4.
Application Context: Highlights increasing responsibility through leading studies (mySkin), co-designing platforms, and supervising students, supporting g.5.
Application Context: Applicant is a medically qualified dermatologist following the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, strongly supporting g.6.
Application Context: Supervisors have extensive track records in psoriasis research, large-scale data, and prediction modelling, supporting ss.1.
Application Context: Detailed descriptions of supervisor roles, meeting frequencies (weekly/fortnightly), and specific training plans (bioinformatics, stats) strongly support ss.2.
Application Context: King's College London and St John's Institute provide world-leading expertise, datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn), and networks, supporting ss.3.
Application Context: Mentions of diverse team composition and inclusive training (DermAcademy) suggest a supportive culture, partially supporting ss.4.
Application Context: The supervisory team includes diverse experts (epidemiology, genetics, statistics, clinical dermatology) with varied backgrounds, supporting wp.2.

Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show a Flesch-Kincaid grade of 7.83, 0% long sentences, and coverage of key elements (problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, impact), strongly supporting pr.1.
